---
title: "Bayesian Statistics"
subtitle: "Updating beliefs with evidence — the one piece of mathematics that doubles as a thinking tool."
---

::: {.field-notes-band}
<div class="fn-eyebrow">Lesson 01 · Track A — Reasoning under uncertainty</div>
<div class="fn-title">Bayesian Statistics: Updating Beliefs with Evidence</div>
<div class="fn-meta">
  <span class="fn-tag">~6 hours · 5 modules</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Prereqs: fractions &amp; algebra</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Interview: base rates, priors</span>
</div>
:::

::: {.lesson-meta}
| | |
|---|---|
| **Audience** | Biologists, bioinformaticians, and quantitative scientists — from advanced undergrad to working researcher |
| **Total time** | ~6 hours across 5 modules, plus the exercise and interview sets |
| **Prerequisites** | Fractions, percentages, basic algebra. Prior probability coursework helps but is **not** required — see the [Background Primer](background.qmd) |
| **Companion pages** | [Background Primer](background.qmd) · [Bayes in Action](bayes-in-action.qmd) · [Interview Problems](interview.qmd) |
:::

## Why this lesson exists

Most people meet Bayes' theorem as a formula to memorize, apply it to two
textbook problems, and forget it. That's a genuine loss, because Bayesian
reasoning is one of the few pieces of mathematics that doubles as a
**general-purpose thinking tool**. It answers a question you face constantly,
in the lab and out of it:

> *I believed something. Then I saw new evidence. What should I believe now?*

Spam filters, medical diagnostics, election forecasts, the false-discovery-rate
correction on your last RNA-seq experiment, the A/B test behind every button on
every major website, the search that finally found Air France 447's wreckage —
all of them run on this one idea. The goal here is not fluency in formalism; it
is to make Bayesian updating feel as natural as arithmetic.

::: {.lens}
This is Lesson 1 for a reason. In a technical interview for a science role, the
Bayesian reflex — *"what's the base rate?"* — is a tell. It separates people who
compute statistics from people who *reason* with them. Nearly every downstream
topic (multiple testing, calibration, classifier precision on rare events) is
this lesson wearing a different costume.
:::

## Learning objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

1. **Explain** the difference between $P(A \mid B)$ and $P(B \mid A)$, and why
   confusing them — the *prosecutor's fallacy* — has produced real wrongful
   convictions.
2. **Compute** posterior probabilities by counting natural frequencies, without
   reaching for the formula.
3. **State and apply** Bayes' theorem in both its probability and odds forms.
4. **Identify** the prior, likelihood, and posterior in a real problem, and
   articulate where the prior comes from and why it matters.
5. **Critique** claims in the news and the scientific literature that quietly
   ignore base rates — including the "significant" result that is probably false
   [@ioannidis2005].
6. **Describe** how modern systems — spam filters, A/B tests, forecasting
   models, genomic FDR control — operationalize Bayesian updating at scale.

## Module breakdown

### Module 1 — The question Bayes answers (45 min)

**Hook:** A rapid antigen test for a disease is "95% accurate." You test
positive. What's the chance you actually have the disease? Nearly everyone —
including a majority of physicians in published surveys [@gigerenzer2007] —
answers "about 95%." The real answer, for a rare disease, can be under 10%.

**Content:**

- Beliefs as probabilities: what it means to say "I'm 70% sure."
- The three ingredients: what you believed before (**prior**), how well the
  evidence fits each hypothesis (**likelihood**), what you believe after
  (**posterior**).
- Base rates and why our brains discard them [@tversky1974].

**Activity:** Estimate answers to three "test result" scenarios by gut feeling,
record them, and revisit at the end of Module 2. The gap between gut and math is
the motivation for everything that follows.

### Module 2 — Counting your way to the answer (60 min)

**Core idea:** Before any formula, solve problems by imagining 10,000 people and
literally counting. This *natural frequencies* approach is the single most
replicated result in risk communication: it measurably improves accuracy over
probability phrasing, for experts and novices alike [@gigerenzer1995].

**Content:**

- The 2×2 breakdown: sick/healthy × positive/negative.
- Working the medical-test problem by counting: 10,000 people, 1% prevalence,
  95% sensitivity, 95% specificity → 95 true positives vs. ~495 false positives
  → posterior ≈ 16%. *(Full walkthrough, plus an
  [interactive explorer](bayes-in-action.qmd#the-interactive-version), in
  [Bayes in Action](bayes-in-action.qmd).)*
- Why false positives dominate when the condition is rare — the **rare-event
  penalty**.
- Re-testing as updating: the posterior from test 1 becomes the prior for test 2.

**Activity:** Small groups solve the breathalyzer and airport-screening problems
by counting, then present their 2×2 tables.

### Module 3 — The theorem itself (60 min)

**Content:**

- Deriving Bayes' theorem in three lines from the definition of conditional
  probability — emphasizing that it is *bookkeeping, not magic*:
  $$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$$
- Mapping each symbol back to the counting exercises: the formula is just the
  2×2 table in compressed notation.
- The **odds form**: posterior odds = prior odds × likelihood ratio. Why
  practitioners — forensic scientists, diagnosticians, forecasters — prefer it:
  evidence becomes a single multiplier.
- Chaining evidence: multiplying likelihood ratios for independent pieces.

**Activity:** Re-solve Module 2's problems with the formula and verify you get
the same answers you got by counting. This "two roads, same destination" step is
deliberately redundant — it is what cements trust in the formula.

### Module 4 — Bayes in the modern world (90 min)

Case studies chosen because the Bayesian machinery is load-bearing, not
decorative. *(Worked numbers for the first two are in
[Bayes in Action](bayes-in-action.qmd).)*

1. **Spam filtering.** Naive Bayes classifiers — how "Congratulations, you've
   won" moves the odds, word by word, via multiplied likelihood ratios. Why the
   "naive" independence assumption is wrong but works anyway.
2. **A/B testing.** How teams decide whether a change actually improves a metric.
   Bayesian A/B testing answers the question people actually ask — "what's the
   probability B beats A?" — which a classical *p*-value does not.
3. **The reproducibility crisis.** Why a field can run thousands of well-powered
   studies and still fill journals with false positives. The base rate of *true*
   hypotheses among those tested is the prior; a "significant" result is the
   evidence; the posterior is the thing that actually matters — and it is often
   alarmingly low [@ioannidis2005]. This is the bridge to
   [Lesson 6 on multiple testing](../../roadmap.qmd#lesson-06).
4. **Finding a lost airplane.** The 2011 search for Air France 447: after two
   years of failure, a Bayesian search analysis combined prior drift models with
   the *failures* of previous searches — absence of evidence as evidence — and
   the wreckage was found within a week of searching the highest-posterior
   region [@stone2014].

**Discussion prompts:** Where does the prior come from in each case? Which case
breaks if the prior is badly wrong? Which self-corrects fastest?

### Module 5 — Priors, controversy, and good judgment (45 min)

**Content:**

- Where priors come from: base rates, historical data, physical models, expert
  judgment — and what to do when reasonable people disagree.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** if your conclusion flips when the prior wiggles, the
  evidence is weak and you should say so out loud. This is the single most
  interview-relevant habit in the lesson.
- The classical vs. Bayesian debate, honestly: what frequentist methods do well,
  why the two usually agree when data is plentiful, and why the difference
  matters most with scarce data or one-off events [@gelman2013].
- Bayesian thinking as epistemic hygiene: "strong opinions, weakly held" has a
  precise mathematical meaning.

**Closing activity:** Revisit your Module 1 gut estimates and write a short
reflection: which intuition was most wrong, and what corrected it?

## Assessment

- **Formative:** the counting activities in Modules 2–3 (instructor circulates,
  checks 2×2 tables).
- **Exercise set:** eight problems on the [Bayes in Action](bayes-in-action.qmd)
  page, from direct computation to critique of a news claim.
- **Interview set:** the [Interview Problems](interview.qmd) page — conceptual
  questions and a "train-of-thought" whiteboard problem, each with a model
  answer.
- **Summative option:** find one real news article or paper that commits
  base-rate neglect or the prosecutor's fallacy and write a one-page Bayesian
  correction with explicit prior, likelihood, and posterior.

## Instructor notes

- The single most common failure mode is introducing the formula too early. Hold
  the line: no formula until Module 3.
- Natural-frequency phrasing ("out of 10,000 people…") reliably beats
  probability phrasing [@gigerenzer1995]. Use it relentlessly.
- If your learners are programmers, the spam-filter case study becomes a
  30-minute build-your-own-naive-Bayes exercise.

## Further reading

Full citations are on every page's reference list. Beyond the primary sources:

- Gigerenzer, G. — *Calculated Risks* (2002). Natural frequencies, book length.
- McGrayne, S. B. — *The Theory That Would Not Die* (2011) [@mcgrayne2011]. The
  history, including the USS *Scorpion* and Enigma.
- Silver, N. — *The Signal and the Noise* (2012). Forecasting and calibration.
- Kurt, W. — *Bayesian Statistics the Fun Way* (2019). A gentle next step.
- 3Blue1Brown — "Bayes theorem, the geometry of changing beliefs" (video). An
  excellent visual companion to Modules 2–3.

::: {.lesson-nav}
<span>← You're at the start of the lesson</span>
<span>[Background Primer →](background.qmd)</span>
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
